text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have now seen what the lampstand was; solid hammered gold, one talent in weight, seven branches rising from one central shaft, covered in almond blossoms in three stages of bud, blossom, and fruit. We have seen how every detail pointed to Christ; the gold to His divine character, the central shaft to His place as the true vine, the almond blossom stages to His birth, baptism, His sacrifice, and His resurrection. Now we come to the question that is most personal. The Kohathites lifted this lampstand onto their shoulders and carried it through the wilderness. We are the new covenant Kohathites; the called-together assembly of God. So what does it mean that we carry the lampstand today?

The lampstand was the only source of light in the Holy Place. Without it, the room was in complete darkness. The priests could not see the table of showbread, could not approach the golden altar, could not serve before God. The light made everything else in the room visible and functional. It was not decorative. It was essential. When the Kohathites lifted the lampstand onto their shoulders and walked out into the wilderness, they were carrying the only light that was inside the Tabernacle.

John 8:12 records the words of Christ Himself: “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.” He came as the true lampstand into the world; the light that the darkness could not overcome (John 1:5). And before He left, He transferred the carrying to us. In Matthew 5:14 He said to His disciples: “You are the light of the world.” Not you were, not you might become; you are. The moment we received Christ, we became carriers of the lampstand. The light that was in Him was placed on our shoulders to be carried into the world.

Paul understood this precisely. In 2 Corinthians 4:6-7 he wrote: “For God, who said, ‘Let light shine out of darkness,’ has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. But we have this treasure in jars of clay, to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us.” The jars of clay represents us; imperfect, wilderness-walking, finite human beings. The treasure is the light of Christ. Paul is saying exactly what the lampstand covered in sea-cow leather was already saying in the wilderness: the most glorious thing in existence is being carried in the most ordinary-looking vessel.

When the Kohathites carried the lampstand, it was covered. First a blue cloth over the golden lamp, and then over that a covering of sea-cow leather; thick, rough, dull, unremarkable. Anyone watching a Kohathite carry that bundle through the desert sand would have seen nothing impressive. Nothing worth stopping for. Nothing that looked like it contained 34 kilograms of hammered gold covered in almond blossoms. From the outside it looked like a plain, rough bundle on a wooden frame. The glory was completely hidden under the ordinary.

This is exactly the experience of those who carry the light of Christ into the world today. The gospel we carry does not always appear impressive to those who are not searching for it. Isaiah 53:2 said of Christ Himself: “He had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him.” The light of God came in flesh & the world hated it. Today the light of God continues to move through us, the vessels the world still hates. Paul wrote in 1 Corinthians 1:18: “For the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.” The gospel sounds like foolishness to the ears of those who are not searching. The sea-cow leather means nothing to them. But to those whose eyes have been opened, to those who are truly searching for the light of God; the covering does not deceive them. They see through the ordinary and find the gold.

Ezekiel 12:2 records Yahweh saying: “Son of man, you dwell in the midst of a rebellious house, who have eyes to see, but see not, who have ears to hear,
but hear not.” The truth has always been covered to those who do not want to see it. Isaiah 6:9-10 speaks of those who hear and do not understand, who see and do not perceive. And yet in Acts 26:18 Christ commissioned Paul to go to the Gentiles “to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light.” The light is covered. But Christ reveals it through us; through our words, our deeds, our actions, and our character; to those who are genuinely searching. The Kohathite simply carried the lampstand faithfully. The light inside was not his to manage. It was his to carry.

This is one of the most important things we must understand about carrying the lampstand. The Kohathite did not produce the light. They did not light the lamps. They did not fill the oil. They did not start the fire. The priests did all of that. The Kohathite simply carried what had already been lit by someone else, covered and prepared by someone else, and entrusted to his shoulders. He was a bearer, not a source.

We are the same. The message we carry is not our own message. The gospel of Christ is not something we invented, improved, or added to. We received it. Paul says in Galatians 1:11-12: “For I would have you know, brothers, that the gospel that was preached by me is not man’s gospel. For I did not receive it from any man, nor was I taught it, but I received it through a revelation of Jesus Christ.” The gospel came from above. The light was lit from above. We carry what we were given. And 2 Corinthians 5:20 says: “We are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us.”
The ambassador does not speak his own words. He delivers the message of the one who sent him. When we speak the truth of the Father, the truth of the Kingdom of Christ, the truth of the gospel; we are not voicing our opinion. We are delivering the message of the King.

In the wilderness, if any Kohathite put the lampstand down and said “this is too heavy for me today” was not simply inconveniencing himself. He was leaving the holy place in darkness. He was failing in the one thing he had been specifically called and numbered to do. In the same way, the new creation believer who puts down the lampstand; who decides the gospel is too heavy to carry, too offensive to share, too costly to bear in a world that finds the sea-cow leather unappealing; is leaving the darkness around them unchanged. Isaiah 62:1 gives us the heart of the true carrier: “For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent, and for Jerusalem’s sake I will not be quiet, until her righteousness goes forth as brightness, and her salvation as a burning torch.” We must not lay it down. We must continue tomorrow.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, You are the Father of lights, and in You there is no shadow of turning (James 1:17). You sent the light of Your Son into the darkness of this world, and You have now placed that same light on our shoulders and said; carry it. Forgive us for the days we have hidden the lampstand under the covering of our own fear and embarrassment, choosing the approval of the world over the calling You placed on our shoulders. Help us to remember that the message is not ours, it is Yours. We are ambassadors, not authors. We are carriers, not creators. Give us courage to carry the lampstand faithfully today; to speak the truth of Your Kingdom, the truth of the gospel of Christ, the truth of who You are as the only Almighty God. And when those around us see only rough leather and find it unimpressive, let us not lose heart. You will open the eyes of those who are truly searching. In Christ’s name, amen.
